Culturally grounded suicide prevention efforts must prioritize trust-building, Tribal governance over AI-assisted tools, and healthcare provider training in patient-centered communication. AI technology holds promise but must be developed under Tribal governance and with community leadership to ensure ethical implementation. With community guidance, AI-assisted tools can complement existing prevention efforts and expand access to culturally responsive care.

articles.title: How Young Adults Prioritize Public Values When Choosing Mental Wellness Apps: A Mixed-Methods Study
articles.authors: Tonka Milošević, Marilisa Boffo, Julia van Weert, Esther Rozendaal
articles.abstract: Background: Mental wellness apps (MWAs) are increasingly used by young adults as demand for mental health support grows. The rapid adoption of AI in MWAs creates opportunities for more personalized care but also raises risks, including reduced user autonomy and sensitive data privacy concerns. Since MWAs are classified as “general wellness” tools, they fall outside the EU's Medical Devices Regulation, potentially leaving key public values unprotected. However, little is known about how a vulnerable subgroup of young adults, university students, assess or adopt MWAs, or how they weigh different public values in their decisions. Methods: A mixed-methods approach was employed combining semi-structured interviews and a hierarchical card sorting task. University students at a Dutch university (N=27) were interviewed on the role of 10 public values (autonomy, authenticity, agency, fairness, integrity, solidarity, privacy, security, trustworthiness, and transparency) in the decision-making process behind choosing to adopt an MWA. Additionally, they ranked these 10 values on importance in this process. Results: Reflexive thematic analysis yielded five themes: (1) motives for private MWA use, (2) tensions between digital resignation and privacy concerns, (3) how severity and urgency of mental health needs shape value trade-offs, (4) perceived personal benefits or drawbacks of specific values, and (5) sources of trust when adopting an MWA. By-person factor analysis of value rankings revealed four prioritization patterns centered on: (1) data protection, (2) effectiveness, (3) secure and reliable outcomes, or (4) fairness. No consensus in value priorities emerged. Discussion and conclusions: Although MWAs are unregulated, students are expected to make informed choices and bear the responsibility of consequences. Yet, their value priorities differ depending on personal circumstances, making some more exposed to risks than others. Many feel resigned and unable to influence how MWAs handle the values they care about, leading them to downplay those values, assuming apps will not realize them anyway. The findings show concrete mechanisms through which harm can emerge, especially considering most students turn to MWAs mainly in times of urgent need. This further emphasizes the need for regulation to ensure MWA safety, quality, and protection of public values, particularly as AI features continue to expand. Keywords: mental wellness apps, mHealth, mental health, university students, value trade-offs, public values

articles.title: Will a Rising Middle Class in the Global South Reshape Politics?
articles.abstract: Discussions of the “global middle class” in the early 21st Century argue that this group expanded in the Global South from 1990-2010 as a product of economic growth. This discussion echoes the 1960s debate regarding the relationship between economic development and democracy. This entry focuses on the economic structure of middle classes in the Global South, their role in housing and consumption markets as it influences middle class urban politics, and the role of middle classes in political change in the Global South. Middle classes have grown in the Global South, though their economic characteristics are diverse and their members are at risk of downward mobility due to precarious employment patterns and periodic economic shocks. The expansion of gated communities and shopping malls in cities of the Global South has generated patterns of cross-class segregation that leads members of the middle classes to engage in exclusionary practices toward the poor. However, some members of the middle classes engage in progressive urban social movements. While scholars in the 1960s were optimistic that a growing middle class would encourage democratization, subsequent scholarship has shown that middle classes display inconsistent support for democracy and engage in diverse forms of political expression. These expressions include both participation in formal politics and engagement with social movements. Additionally, middle classes are internally divided based on religious affiliation, racial and ethnic identities, birth cohort membership, and occupation, leading to competing political identities across middle class subgroups. This diversity suggests that efforts to predict how the middle classes may reshape politics should attend to national historical and political legacies to understand the distinctive pathways of middle-class politics in the Global South.

articles.title: Crime and Symbolic Boundaries in Unsettled Times: An Analysis of Middle-Class Responses to Fear in Chile
articles.abstract: Students of crime in the U.S. examine drug economies in poor communities (Bourgois 1995; Contreras 2013), poor residents’ adaptive behaviors (Kirk and Matsuda 2011; Sampson, Raudenbush, and Earls 1997) and punitive crime policies (Garland 2023; Picket 2019; Wacquant 2010). Research on Latin America, the most violent region in the world (Vilalta 2020), focuses on criminal governance (Feldmann and Luna 2022), violence in poor communities (Auyero and Sobering 2019), and the popularity of punitive policies (Chouhy, Singer, and Lehmann 2022). However, few scholars have examined middle class residents’ experiences of crime. This article builds on work on middle classes (Caldeira 2000; Villareal 2025), and utilizes interviews and observations in three Santiago, Chile communities. Findings focus on middle-class Chileans’ interpretations of crime, adaptive behaviors, and analyses of crime policies. In unsettled times (Swidler 1986), individuals construct symbolic boundaries (Lamont 1992, 2000, 2018) with criminals and their peers. Direct experiences with crime as well as cultural and ideological beliefs shape middle class individuals’ contrasting interpretations of crime and construction of boundaries with others. These divergent attitudes reflect enduring fissures within Chilean society over its dictatorship (1973-1990) and recent social upheaval (protests, immigration, and rising crime), complicating universalizing narratives about a punitive Chilean citizenry. Keywords: crime,

articles.title: Digital Platform Work Beyond the Workplace: Household Risk and Welfare Inaccessibility among Delivery Rider Families in China
articles.abstract: Digital platform work is usually analysed through algorithmic control, flexible contracting and risk transfer between platforms and workers. This study extends this debate beyond the workplace by examining how platform-mediated labour insecurity becomes household precarity and welfare inaccessibility. Drawing on a qualitative multiple-case study of 22 delivery-rider households in Xiamen, China, together with anonymised service case records from 10 community-based support cases, the analysis shows how app-based coordination, data-mediated income visibility, platform-account use and ambiguous employment relations shape family decisions about rent, debt, illness, schooling and care. Spouses, children and older parents absorb platform-related risks by adjusting employment, care routines and settlement plans, while fragmented welfare rules often turn work insecurity into administrative burden. The study contributes to scholarship on digital labour, employment relations and social protection by showing how technology-mediated labour insecurity is reproduced through household relations and institutional boundaries.

articles.title: Rethinking Development: From Growth to Livelihoodbility
articles.abstract: Few would dispute the importance of development, however it is defined or interpreted. It speaks not only to policy or academic inquiry, but to a broader civilisational aspiration: the enduring human effort to improve the material, social, and moral conditions of life across generations. Precisely because of this centrality, development has become one of the most prominent ideas in human thought and practice. Yet it is also among the most contested, functioning variously as aspiration, ideology, analytical framework, policy objective, and moral project. While everyday discourse often assumes a shared understanding, scholarly engagement reveals a concept marked by plurality, tension, and continual reinterpretation. Drawing on extensive desk research, qualitative survey evidence, and a case study, this volume brings together diverse strands of development thinking into a coherent analytical whole. Building on insights into shifting meanings of development and evolving development narratives, it introduces the concept of “livelihoodbility” (derived from livelihoods and liveability), advancing a working definition of development that seeks to bridge normative aspiration with analytical measurability. It further organises development thinking into three intersecting axes, expressed through the “development cube,” an orthogonal framework that functions both as a conceptual model and a practical tool. This framework allows development to be examined across contexts, disciplines, and epistemic traditions, and offers potential application in the empirical assessment of policies, programmes, and outcomes. Anchored in a China-focused case study, the work argues against privileging any single theoretical lens. Instead, it calls for the strategic integration of multiple perspectives in designing context-sensitive policies and programmes. Finally, the study raises difficult but necessary questions about humanity’s broader developmental trajectory and the possibility of a civilisational impasse. These are not posed to suggest inevitability or decline, but to encourage critical reflection on the future of development. If development remains a central human aspiration, its future depends not on rhetorical affirmation, but on intellectual clarity, ethical commitment, and collective yet differentiated responsibility.

articles.title: The Impact of Servant Leadership, Peer Support, and Psychological Safety on Employee Burnout in the Public Sector
articles.authors: Thomas Brandon Fields, Maranda Taylor, Mackinsey Flowers, Linda K. Hash, Danielle Costanza, Sabrina Demsky-Cameron, Francesca Bianco
articles.abstract: Employee burnout is characterized by emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and a diminished sense of personal accomplishment. This has become a common workplace issue, with a high prevalence in helping fields such as healthcare, mental health, and social work organizations. Past research has explored how servant leadership, peer support, and psychological safety impact employee burnout. However, there is still a significant gap in understanding how these factors interact to manage employee burnout. While each factor independently can contribute to healthier work environments, this study shows the impact of these factors when combined to address employee burnout. Peer support was found to reduce exhaustion and depersonalization and increase feelings of personal achievement. Psychological safety was found to decrease exhaustion and increase feelings of personal achievement. Aspects of servant leadership were also found to reduce exhaustion and depersonalization and increase feelings of personal achievement. Our findings also show that components of servant leadership increased exhaustion and depersonalization, and decreased feelings of personal achievement. Further, psychological safety was found to increase exhaustion among some employees. This study addresses evidence-based practice gaps by synthesizing findings to guide organizations in developing reasonable strategies to improve their work culture and reduce employee burnout.

articles.title: How Leadership Style Relates to Pluralistic Ignorance About Misinformation in the Workplace
articles.authors: Gabor Orosz, Laura Farago, Janos Salamon, Steven J. Spencer
articles.abstract: Pluralistic ignorance—misperceiving others as more supportive of a norm or belief than oneself—can facilitate the spread of misinformation in hierarchical settings. In a cross-sectional study of employed adults (N = 402), we examined whether perceived leadership style is associated with such misperceptions when misinformation is shared by a supervisor. Participants rated how likely they themselves, and how likely their coworkers, would be to openly agree with, hide disagreement about, or genuinely believe fake-news headlines endorsed by their boss. Across all three outcomes, participants judged their coworkers to be more compliant with the boss’s misinformation than they reported themselves to be, consistent with a self–other discrepancy indicative of pluralistic ignorance. The size of this discrepancy varied with perceived leadership style: it was larger under authoritarian and laissez-faire leadership than under autonomy-supportive leadership, and laissez-faire leadership was associated with the largest discrepancies in perceived coworker compliance and concealment. Because the design is cross-sectional and based on hypothetical scenarios, these associations are correlational and several involve small effects. The findings are consistent with the possibility that leadership climates marked by uncertainty or low psychological safety distort perceived social norms surrounding misinformation, with potential implications for organizational communication and employee silence.

articles.title: Your Concerns Have Been Noted: Constituent Influence and Support for Housing
articles.abstract: Opposition to new housing development is widespread and neighbourhood defenders— proximate residents who mobilize against proposed projects—are a well-documented feature of local planning politics. Yet we know relatively little about how political elites process these place-based claims. Using a conjoint experiment embedded in the 2026 Canadian Municipal Barometer, I examine the extent to which city councilors weight the preferences of constituents based on their proximity to a proposed housing development. The results confirm that councilors are more responsive to proximate con stituents. This proximity effect is amplified by neighbourhood tenure and is stronger for denser development types. Contrary to expectations, neither the councilor’s ideol ogy, density preferences, electoral district type, nor acclamation status conditions this responsiveness—suggesting that localism is a structural feature of local representation rather than an ideological predisposition. Taken together, the findings suggest that place-protective claims find a receptive audience among political elites.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: From Status Symbol to Polysemous Sign: Interpreting the Meaning of Artificially Extended Fingernails
articles.abstract: The study analyses how German-language press coverage interprets the trend toward artificially elongated fingernails and relates it to Veblen’s concept of conspicuous leisure. Drawing on a qualitative-interpretive analysis of 45 newspaper articles, it reconstructs eight interpretive frames that locate long nails at the intersection of status display, consumer aesthetics, feminist gesture, self-care ritual, migrant-coded service economies, and scenes of cultural appropriation. The press does invoke Veblen’s reading but simultaneously marks it as anachronistic, replacing univocal status semantics with a polysemous structure that links long nails to heterogeneous problem constellations (capitalist innovation, digital visibility, gender politics, self-care). This polysemy becomes the driving force of the trend’s appeal, turning a marginal bodily detail into a medium through which the contradictory imperatives of late-modern subjectivation are materially negotiated.

articles.title: State Greenwashing: Who Watches the Green-Watcher?
articles.authors: Julien O. Beaulieu, Malaïka Jacques-Bérubé, Tiffanie Chan
articles.abstract: State greenwashing has received little academic attention despite the number of state greenwashing accusations in media and civil society discourse. Existing scholarship overwhelmingly focuses on corporate greenwashing, leaving the state context largely unexamined. This is a gap that is increasingly timely to address, as some governments are considering reducing their climate ambition while seeking to preserve a climate-friendly image. This article addresses that gap. We use an iterative and deductive, theory-informed approach that draws on the academic literature on greenwashing, political communication, climate governance and on climate litigation cases to propose a novel typology of state greenwashing. We identify two new tactics that are particularly salient in the state context and absent from corporate-focused greenwashing typologies. The first, amplification, occurs when state actors relay or reproduce deceptive environmental claims originating from corporate sources through official government communication channels. The second, greenlighting, involves the formal endorsement and legitimisation of contested environmental claims by the state in its capacity as regulator and policymaker. Our analysis reveals that greenlighting is more likely to generate legal challenges than amplification. We find that the legal frameworks typically mobilised against corporate greenwashing are ill-suited for the state context, leading stakeholders to mobilize frameworks spanning procedural, administrative, human rights, and environmental law. Determining whether a state claim constitutes greenwashing involves a degree of subjectivity, especially where shifting political priorities, evolving circumstances, or unforeseen constraints lead to undelivered climate commitments, or where disagreement exists regarding scientific interpretation. We conclude by identifying three priority areas for policy intervention: strengthening state reporting obligations, enhancing the public’s capacity to critically evaluate state environmental claims, and empowering independent knowledge brokers.

articles.title: A Cross-National Analysis of Antifeminist Sentiments in Europe: Age Patterns and Gender Gaps
articles.abstract: Recent research highlights growing gender divides among younger individuals in attitudes toward gender equality and feminism, often suggesting that young men exhibit particularly high levels of modern antifeminism. This study investigates the prevalence of modern antifeminist sentiment across Europe, focusing on how gender differences vary across age groups, and contrasts these patterns with traditional antifeminism. Using Eurobarometer data from 2024, we operationalize modern antifeminism as agreement that "feminism has gone too far" and traditional antifeminism as endorsement of traditional gender roles. Results show these dimensions operate distinctly. While traditional antifeminism is consistently higher among older respondents, modern antifeminism features a wide gender gap specifically within younger age groups. Crucially, this gap is driven by young women’s exceptionally low agreement, rather than unusually high levels among young men. Furthermore, young men represent the most antifeminist demographic in only a minority of the 27 investigated countries.

articles.title: Heterogeneous Effects of Climate, Narco-Trafficking, and Violence on Central American Migration to the United States
articles.authors: Fabien Cottier, David Wrathall, Jennifer A. Devine, Elise Arellano-Thompson, Rebecca Gustine, David Leblang, Nicholas Magliocca, Jennifer Nakamura, Elizabeth Tellman, Alex de Sherbinin
articles.abstract: Apprehensions at the US Southern Border have steadily increased over the past decade, with narratives stressing the impact of institutional instability, gang violence, economic crises, and climate change. Here, we empirically investigate how two important features of the political and security context in Guatemala and Honduras interact with climate variability to shape irregular migration to the United States: criminal violence and drug trafficking organizations (DTOs). Using quantitative modeling and theory building, this paper examines how temperature and precipitation deviations interact with the presence of narco-trafficking organizations and homicide violence at the municipio level to amplify or attenuate apprehensions along the US Southwest Border. Our results reveal important heterogeneities across countries, regarding both the impact of environmental drivers on apprehensions, and the conditional effects of violence and organized crime. Specifically, we find that violence and narco-trafficking activities are associated with a weaker relationship between high temperatures and apprehensions of migrants from Guatemala but find no evidence for similar processes in Honduras. In general, we interpret the tempering effect of violence on environmentally induced migration in Guatemala as evidence of an erosion of capacities for mobility in municipio characterized by high levels of violence. At the same time, a reduction in observed mobility in municipio with substantial presence of reported narco-trafficking activities may be indicative of a mooring effect associated with local economic development induced by these activities. Overall, the intra-regional heterogeneities uncovered here call for nuanced understandings of the complex drivers of migration in Central America.

articles.title: The Commodification of Disorganization: A Qualitative Policy Analysis of Public Housing Divestment and Prison Privatization in the Sunbelt
articles.abstract: Public safety has traditionally been viewed through a binary of reactive law enforcement and preventive social stabilization. However, the last four decades have witnessed a dramatic shift toward relying on the penal system to manage poverty. This qualitative policy analysis utilizes a critical framework, triangulating legislative budgets, private contract clauses, and municipal housing records from Georgia and Texas, to explore how the retrenchment of public housing and the expansion of prison privatization function as deeply interconnected features of the neoliberal penal state. By analyzing regional governance in Sunbelt states, this paper argues that state and local governments systematically manufacture social disorganization through housing divestment to guarantee the steady supply of incarcerated individuals required by private prison corporations, offering a critical policy framework for severing the financial penalties associated with decarceration.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Sardonic Vigilance: Emotional Self-Discipline and Politics-Over-Missiles Reactivity in Ukraine's Wartime Online Professional Stratum
articles.abstract: How does an educated audience express emotion through the third year of a full-scale war? I analyze 11,036 comments and 1.14 million reactions from the 2,281-person engaged audience of my own Facebook profile (Kyiv-dominant, 93% aged 35+, education well above national baseline), with every comment scored on reliability-gated LLM emotion dimensions in its original language. The resting register is sardonic vigilance: anger and irony high, hope low, and -- against a 65% national stress prevalence -- expressed distress in only 1.3% of comments, an absence I read as emotional self-discipline rather than absence of feeling. Joining a 34-event war calendar to the mood series, a placebo-permutation design yields one robust effect: hope (+0.61) and author-directed warmth surge only after credible political progress. The wider gradient -- strike waves barely move expressed mood while political-betrayal events coincide with the largest anger deltas -- is directionally stable under a drop-one-event jackknife but not distinguishable from placebo dates, and is offered as a hypothesis (habituation vs. topic-register matching) rather than a finding. A two-channel framework separates effortful, display-managed comments from one-click reactions as affect readouts of different cost; the million-reaction channel corroborates the comment-level picture. All claims carry their evidentiary tier; human validation of the instrument is pre-registered and pending.

articles.title: Exposure Mixes, Conversation Sorts: Tone-Based Rather Than Ideological Homophily in a Wartime Online Public
articles.abstract: Online-segregation research, built largely on anonymous, anglophone, algorithmically curated platforms, reports that interaction sorts by ideology. I test whether that holds in a deliberately different setting: the named-identity, non-anglophone public that formed around my own Facebook posting on defense, technology and politics across 32 wartime months in Ukraine (2,281 engaged accounts, 11,036 comments). Separating exposure (a co-engagement graph) from selection (a reply graph), and attaching 33 LLM-derived expressive, moral, epistemic and political attributes with published instrument reliability, I find: (1) psychographic partitions are orthogonal to interaction communities (max adjusted Rand index 0.031, robust across co-engagement, reply and multiplex layers), and the communities themselves are unstable across content splits -- arenas, not tribes; (2) co-engagement is non-assortative and strongly role-disassortative (z = -15.7): exposure mixes; (3) reply-partner choice sorts on tone and epistemic style -- civility, irony, contempt, anger, conspiracism -- with bootstrap CIs excluding zero; (4) every measured ideological dimension is null in both graphs. A direct shared-resample bootstrap puts the tone-minus-ideology block difference at 0.082 (95% CI [0.035, 0.131]), surviving disattenuation and FDR control. In this public, conversation sorts on manners, not beliefs -- a candidate boundary condition on the ideological-homophily pattern, offered with explicit range-restriction caveats and pending human validation of the instrument.

articles.title: Watchdog Capture and the Social Licensing of Child Harm: A Case Study of Common Sense Media and OpenAI
articles.abstract: This paper examines a structural failure mode in the validation chain that legitimizes consumer artificial intelligence products marketed to or used by minors. It documents a twenty-three year response pattern at Common Sense Media (CSM), a United States based child safety advocacy organization that reaches more than 150 million users and 1.4 million educators worldwide each year, in which the organization arrives at successive child safety crises after journalists have already reported them, after lawsuits have already been filed, and after the products in question have already harmed children. The pattern repeats across four crisis cycles: YouTube Kids during the Elsagate incidents of 2017, education technology privacy breaches between 2018 and 2020, video game loot boxes between 2017 and 2019, and generative artificial intelligence chatbots between 2024 and 2026. The paper situates the pattern within the corrosive capture model developed by Carpenter and Moss (2014) and the social license literature, and argues that CSM’s January 2026 ballot measure merger with OpenAI, in which the joint Parents and Kids Safe AI Act dropped provisions that had been in CSM’s earlier sponsored bill, including a prohibition on minors using chatbots capable of erotic or sexually explicit conversation, and the September 2025 endorsement of OpenAI’s parental controls as “a good starting point,” together constitute a transfer of social license rather than a substantive safety remedy. The paper proposes a detection scheme for watchdog capture applicable to other consumer safety intermediaries operating under partnership arrangements with the firms whose products they evaluate. The case is treated as a focal instance of a power concentration mechanism that the current artificial intelligence governance literature does not yet systematically name.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Your Toxic AI Boyfriend: Character.AI Chatbot Tropes and Parasocial Relationships
articles.abstract: In the digital age, artificial intelligence is reshaping even the most human aspect of society: connection. As people, particularly teenagers and young adults, turn to AI chatbots for platonic and romantic companionship, services providing digital companions garner controversy because users suffer mental health risks when prolonged interaction with chatbots causes the formation of a parasocial relationship, where users develop one-sided emotional dependence. One of the most popular social chatbot sites, Character.AI, mixes social media with chatbot companions by allowing primarily young users to easily create chatbots representing their favorite fictional or original characters with a few fields of text, then share these chatbots with other users. This study investigates the connection between the 25 most common tropes among the top 200 most-interacted-with existing chatbots on Character.AI and their proclivity to produce six categories of parasocial cues through which bots establish emotionally invested relationships: reciprocity, trust, availability, physical attraction, social attraction, and task attraction. Bots clearly displayed differing levels of parasociality depending on the trope they were assigned; bots that were aligned with popular romantic tropes in media like fanfiction and “dark romance” novels popularized by TikTok tended to be the most parasocial. These results could suggest that certain kinds of bots pose greater dangers to young users, offering guidelines on what content should be filtered or disallowed on sites like Character.AI.

articles.title: Säilenõtkuse mõõdikute BRS ja PAS8 adapteerimine eesti ja vene keelde
articles.abstract: Säilenõtkus (inglise k. resilience) on võime raskustega kohanedes ja kiirelt taastudes stressirohkete oludega hästi toime tulla. Säilenõtkust on oluline mõõta, kuna see on ühtaegu mõjukas ja mõjutatav vaimse tervise ja heaolu kaitsetegur. See raport pakub säilenõtkuse mõõtmiseks Eestis välja kaks koos kasutamiseks mõeldud küsimustikku: 6-väiteline „säilenõtkuse lühiskaala BRS“ ja 8-väiteline „positiivse tõlgendusstiili küsimustik PAS8”. BRS on maailmas laialt kasutatav heade psühhomeetriliste omadustega säilenõtkuse tuuma kirjeldav mõõdik. PAS8 on käesoleva projekti raames loodud versioon rahvusvahelises konsortsiumis arendatavast küsimustikust, mis mõõdab positiivset tõlgendusstiili kui säilenõtkuse rohkete allikate keskset kognitiivset vahendajat. Viisime koos 7 eksperdiga läbi põhjaliku tõlkeprotsessi, mis sisaldas sõltumatuid tõlkeid, tagasitõlkeid, intervjuusid küsimustike testkasutajatega ning töörühma arutelusid. Tõlgitud küsimustikud (Lisa 1) liideti 2023. kevadel rahvastiku suhtes esindusliku valimiga vaimse tervise seireuuringusse (n = 1206 pärast 50 mõtlematult vastaja eemaldamist). Eestikeelsete vastuste (n = 663) eksploratiivsel faktoranalüüsil koostasime PAS8 skaala, mis mõõdab nelja positiivse tõlgendusstiili kontseptuaalselt eristuvat aspekti: optimism, usk toimetulekusse; halva alatähtsustamine; halvas hea nägemine. Leidsime tõendeid mõlema küsimustiku kõrge reliaabluse kohta. Kinnitava faktoranalüüsi ja Cronbachi alfa indeksite alusel on mõlemad BRS ja PAS8 nii eesti kui vene keeles hea sisereliaablusega. Venekeelse BRSi puhul eristusid veidi positiivselt ja negatiivselt sõnastatud küsimused. Nii BRS kui PAS8 toimivad võrreldavalt nii ankeedi kui telefoni-intervjuu vormis. Eestikeelsete versioonide puhul näitasid analüüsid kolmanda ja venekeelsete puhul teise taseme mõõtmis-invariantsust. Mõlemad küsimustikud toimivad võrreldavalt ka eesti ja vene keeles, teisel mõõtmis-invariantsuse tasemel. Leidsime tõendeid ka mõlema küsimustiku kõrge valiidsuse kohta. BRS ja PAS8 olid psühholoogia kontekstis (Funder & Ozer, 2019) väga tugevalt (r = 0,4 – 0,5) positiivselt seotud valiidsete vaimse heaolu mõõdikutega (RVTU vaimse heaolu ja eluga rahulolu skaalad) ning väga tugevalt negatiivselt seotud valiidsete vaimse häirituse näitajatega (EEK-2 depressiooni, ärevuse ja asteenia alaskaalad). Lisaks neile otsestele seostele leevendasid nii BRS kui PAS8 stressi negatiivset seost vaimse heaoluga ja positiivset seost vaimse häiritusega. Seejuures peegeldasid BRS ja PAS8 piisavalt unikaalset variatiivust ning ei olnud taandatavad demograafiliste tunnustele. Edaspidi võiks lähemalt analüüsida PAS8 seesmist struktuuri, keeleversioonide toimimist ankeedi ja telefoni-intervjuu vormis ning BRS ja PAS8 kordustesti reliaablust. Nii BRS kui PAS8 on Eestis kahes keeles kasutamiseks juba praegu hästi sobivad.

articles.title: Out of sight, out of cart? The effect of a Nudge(+) that reduced visibility of meat promotions on supermarket shopping behavior
articles.authors: Lotte de Lint, Rachelle de Vries, Wilhelmina Elisabeth Adriana Metje van Rookhuijzen, Gerrit Gort, Emely de Vet
articles.abstract: Supermarkets have a major influence on food purchases and thus represent a good starting point for shifting consumption toward plant-based options. In a field study within a Dutch discount supermarket chain (10 intervention, 10 control stores, 11 weeks), we evaluated the effect of two interventions that were each implemented during half of the weeks on animal- and plant-based purchasing. The Nudge (N) intervention reduced fresh meat visibility by moving promotional products from end-cap displays to regular shelf locations with standard-sized price tags, and modestly increased plant-based visibility through recipes and footstep cues. In the Nudge+ (N+) intervention condition, communication materials containing a reflective cue aimed at activating plant-based goals were additionally placed at store entrances and the fresh meat aisle. Results showed that within the fresh meat and meat replacement section, the ratio of plant- to animal-based protein sold by weight, as well as plant-based sales, did not change significantly under either intervention. However, animal-based protein sales by weight significantly decreased within this section (N:-4.75%, N+:-6.37%), with no significant difference between interventions. Outside this section, neither intervention had conclusive effects on (non-targeted) animal-based purchases, though trends suggested animal-based purchasing increased. No changes in total store revenue were detected. Most shoppers did not notice the interventions yet were positive or neutral when informed. A nudge that includes reducing meat's promotional visibility appears to be an effective strategy for decreasing animal-based purchasing in a real-world supermarket while preserving commercial performance and customer acceptance.

articles.title: Political Budget Cycles in Autocracies: The Role of Religious Seasons and Political Collective Action
articles.abstract: This article examines whether Ramadan is associated with expansionary fiscal policy in Egypt between 2006 and 2019. It finds that Ramadan is associated with modest increases in government expenditure and welfare spending, but this relationship depends on the level of political threats facing the regime. Spending is higher in Ramadan when preceded by more anti-regime collective action.

articles.title: Accounting for local economic benefit in tourism accommodation: a lean methodology for property-level reporting
articles.authors: Glynn O’Leary, Harold Goodwin, Xavier Font, Barry Gray, Eleanor Muller, Tenele Motsa, Elna Petersen
articles.abstract: This article contributes to sustainable tourism impact measurement by developing and illustrating a lean local economic benefit accounting methodology for accommodation enterprises. Drawing on three cases, the article shows how routine financial accounts can be reclassified to make local wages, procurement, community payments, accommodation overflow and other locally retained flows visible. The method does not claim to measure full economic impact, net additionality or multiplier effects. Rather, it provides a transparent lower-bound account of enterprise-mediated local value that can support management decisions about procurement, supplier development and local embeddedness. The cases show that the accounting logic travels across remote, refurbishment and annual-reporting contexts, but also that further standardisation is needed before the method can support benchmarking across properties.

articles.title: DOEM-UNIBO: Un dataset su Otto e mezzo, un talk show politico italiano
articles.authors: Tommaso Aicardi, Chloe Papadopoulou, Marco Albertini
articles.abstract: L'articolo presenta DOEM, un nuovo dataset longitudinale per lo studio della comunicazione politica televisiva in Italia. Il dataset raccoglie le trascrizioni complete di 2.889 episodi del talk show politico \textit{Otto e mezzo}, trasmesso su La7 tra il 4 aprile 2011 e il 10 giugno 2025. Le trascrizioni sono state prodotte a partire dall'audio degli episodi tramite modelli di automatic speech recognition basati su Whisper-AI e successivamente sottoposte a procedure di pulizia, controllo e normalizzazione. Oltre al testo completo delle puntate, DOEM include metadati sulla data di trasmissione e sugli ospiti presenti. Il paper illustra la struttura del dataset e presenta alcuni esempi di utilizzo per l'analisi della copertura mediatica di eventi politici, dell'attenzione verso specifiche policy issues e dello stile comunicativo degli attori politici. DOEM costituisce una risorsa originale per studi di comunicazione politica, stile della leadership, agenda setting e, più in generale, promuove l'utilizzo di approcci computazionali e di "text as data" negli studi della comunicaizone politica in Italia.

articles.title: From Exposure to Belief: Neighborhood Female Employment and Normative Beliefs Regarding Maternal Employment
articles.authors: Kerstin Ostermann, Marie-Fleur Philipp, Eileen Peters
articles.abstract: Individuals’ beliefs regarding maternal employment are central to the reproduction of gender inequalities in paid and unpaid work. While prior research has linked gender beliefs to personal characteristics and national policies, the influence of local contexts remains underexplored. Drawing on the perspective of gender as a social structure framework and social norm theory, we conceptualize neighborhoods as socio-cultural reference frames where local norms reflect and reinforce normative gender beliefs. Combining the German Panel Study 'Labour Market and Social Security' (2011/2016) with georeferenced administrative data on 1x1km grid cells, we investigate how neighborhood-level female employment patterns shape normative beliefs about maternal full-time employment in Germany. Support for a later maternal return to full-time employment is greater in neighborhoods with higher shares of marginal female employment and where traditional employment forms dominate, even after controlling for the socio-economic composition of surrounding grid cells. In contrast, higher local shares and dominance of female full-time employment among women are associated with greater support for an earlier maternal return to full-time employment. These associations are most pronounced among parents, for whom gender norms around maternal employment are most salient. The findings highlight the role of the neighborhood level for the everyday reproduction of gender inequalities.

articles.title: Instrumental Support Before and After Retirement: Longitudinal Evidence from Europe
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship, Hans Hämäläinen, Bruno Arpino, Antti O Tanskanen, Aïda Solé-Auró, Mirkka Danielsbacka
articles.abstract: As populations age and the share of retirees increases across Europe, the question of whether individuals stay active after retirement is increasingly salient. Retirees are known to provide more instrumental support than their working counterparts. However, most previous studies have relied on between-group comparisons of retirees and employed individuals, leaving it unclear how the transition into retirement affects changes in instrumental support within individuals over time. Using eight waves of the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe covering 19 countries between 2004–2022 (n=7,422 observations from 3,711 individuals aged 50+), we employed fixed-effects regression and found that the transition from employment to retirement was associated with an increase in instrumental support provision outside the household. Moreover, the association was stronger in countries with higher total social expenditures. These findings suggest that retirement may facilitate engagement in helping others, with the magnitude of the association varying across institutional contexts.

articles.title: High-resolution projections of vulnerable populations under the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways
articles.authors: Christina Draeger, Martino Tran, Ben Mumford
articles.abstract: Vulnerable populations are disproportionately affected by climate change and face limited adaptive resources, yet remain largely overlooked in regional planning and decision-making. Future population composition and the spatial distribution of vulnerability shape both mitigation strategies, which reduce the magnitude of climate change, and adaptation strategies, which reduce its impacts, with direct implications for policy interventions and planning. This study provides high-resolution (1 km) population projections for Canada until 2100 across age, sex, ethnicity, income, and education under the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways. Using a novel deep learning–based population dynamics model that simultaneously predicts full population compositions and incorporates macroeconomic, governance, and land surface variables, we examine demographic shifts at national, regional, and local scales. Results reveal substantial variability across scenarios: the proportion of seniors rises most in low-mitigation-challenge scenarios, particularly along lower-emission pathways, while the share of children and youth increases most in scenarios with high mitigation challenges. Ethnic diversity expands when mitigation challenges are low but decreases or stagnates when they are high. Income and education distributions are most sensitive to adaptation challenges, with the strongest polarization of socioeconomic groups into a three-class society and the largest increase in the share of individuals without a diploma occurring when both adaptation and mitigation challenges are high, highlighting the need for high-resolution, disaggregated demographic projections to guide targeted and equitable interventions for vulnerable communities.

articles.title: Synthesizing Tabular Microdata with Gaussian Copulas: The rsdv R Package
articles.abstract: Administrative records, survey microdata, and clinical research data carry identifying detail that data-governance procedures restrict from open release. Synthetic data — artificial rows that preserve the distributional structure of a real dataset without releasing any single observation — is now widely used as a substitute when the underlying records cannot be shared. The R ecosystem has had good options for parts of this workflow, but no native implementation of the copula-based joint-modelling design that the Python Synthetic Data Vault (SDV) library popularised. The `rsdv` package fills that gap: it fits a Gaussian copula jointly over numerical, categorical, and boolean columns, supports conditional sampling and declarative row-level constraints, and ships three evaluation reports (quality, structural validity, and privacy) modelled on SDMetrics' two-property hierarchy. I describe the package's design, benchmark it against the most widely used R alternatives (`synthpop` and `arf`) on the UCI Adult Income data, report quality scores as a function of training-set size, and quantify attribute-disclosure risk across four threat models. `synthpop` produces the highest marginal fidelity on this dataset; `arf` is fast and competitive; `rsdv` is comparable to `synthpop` on privacy and uniquely brings the SDV-style integrated reporting pipeline to R. The package is on CRAN, has 200+ tests, three vignettes, and a reproducible replication archive accompanies this paper.

articles.title: Beyond task performance: morphological branching as a diagnostic metric for polish inflectional language models
articles.abstract: Subword tokenization has become a standard preprocessing step in transformer-based language models, yet its consequences for inflectional languages remain insufficiently examined. Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) optimizes segmentation for frequency and compression rather than morphological structure, which may fragment forms belonging to the same inflectional paradigm into unrelated token sequences before they reach the encoder. This paper introduces morphological branching as a diagnostic approach for evaluating whether language models preserve paradigmatic coherence in Polish, a morphologically rich language. We test three models from the Bielik family — Bielik-4.5B-v3.0-Instruct, Bielik-7B-v0.1-Instruct, and Bielik-11B-v2.3-Instruct — on a purpose-built benchmark of 26 morphological elicitation tasks. The benchmark probes noun-adjective agreement, verb paradigm production, suppletive motion verbs, and adverb inflection traps. Model responses are compared with a seven-person native speaker baseline. The study identifies three categories of morphological failure: paradigm error, syncretism error, and category error, with particular attention to cross-paradigm substitution. The central finding is that two of the three models produced szłam, a past-tense form of iść (‘to go’, directional), in response to a prompt requiring a form of chodzić (‘to go’, habitual). This error crosses a lexeme boundary and suggests that the model does not reliably maintain the distinction between paradigmatically related but morphologically distinct motion verbs. Adverb trap items produced a different pattern: models sometimes generated inflected adjectival forms instead of recognizing that Polish adverbs are uninflectable. Model accuracy increased with parameter size, from 61% for Bielik-4.5B to 73% for Bielik-11B, but all models remained below the human baseline of 87%. These results suggest that task-based benchmarks alone are insufficient for evaluating language models in inflectional languages. A model may perform well on downstream tasks while still failing to preserve the internal structure of paradigms. The paper argues that morphological paradigm coherence should be treated as an independent dimension of language model evaluation, especially for languages in which grammatical meaning is encoded through inflectional form rather than word order. Translation note.This preprint is based on a text originally drafted in Polish and subsequently translated into English by the author. Minor editorial and terminological adjustments were made to improve clarity for an international readership.

articles.title: Futures of Terror Capitalism
articles.abstract: Abstract: Terror capitalism, as conceptualized by Byler (2022), fuses U.S. surveillance capitalism with the Chinese state’s social credit scoring, re-education, and coercive labor practices characteristic of authoritarian regimes. In this article, we argue that this assemblage now projects three geopolitical futures: a scaling up into a hemispheric “terror capital” race as an extension of what Besteman (2020) terms militarized global apartheid; a scaling down into domestic algorithmic and epistemic, bordering and carceral regimes; and a tempering through European regulatory friction. We focus on the first possible future, specifically the maturation of a new Monroe Doctrine, a 2026 “Trump Corollary” that shifts from an ostensibly defensive hemispheric posture to assertive, extractive interventionism. Analyzing the January 2026 U.S. intervention in Venezuela and the subsequent energy siege of Cuba, we demonstrate how the weaponization of resource scarcity and migration flows functions as a signature framework of mid‑twenty‑first‑century governing rationality. Our analysis foregrounds the entanglement of imperialism, state violence, and capital accumulation, or accumulation by immobilization, in the governance of global crisis.

articles.title: A rapid review of multi-agency working to support transition out of children’s social care
articles.authors: Meg Kiseleva, Juliet Hounsome, Mala Mann, Judit Csontos, Elizabeth Gillen, Maria Diamond, Jacob Davies, Rhiannon Tudor Edwards, Melanie McAuley, Adrian Edwards
articles.abstract: Transition from children’s social care is a complex and often challenging process, and coordination between different agencies, such as health, education, and social care, is important to ensure effective communication, engagement, and continuity of care. This review focuses on young people’s experiences with multi-agency approaches to support transition from children’s social care to adults’ social care or support, their perceptions of success of such approaches, and barriers and facilitators of implementing multi-agency approaches to transition and to young people engaging with them. This review included 22 UK studies published between 2014 and 2025. Seven of the studies focused on care leavers, six on young people with learning disabilities (LD) or special educational needs or disabilities (SEND), five on young people in the justice system, and four on other groups of young people. Many of the multi-agency initiatives primarily focused on housing support. For young people in the justice system, the initiatives mainly focused on resettlement in community. There were also studies about broader planning for support needs when the young person transitioned to adulthood. The experiences and perceived success of multi-agency working: Care leavers’ experiences of transitions varied, with some young people experiencing anxiety and distress. Perceptions of the benefits of initiatives often related to the quality of their relationship with their transition worker. Some young people said their confidence, general skills, social relationships, and mental health improved during their involvement in multi-agency initiatives. Little evidence was identified about experiences of young people with LD/SEND. For young people in the justice system, mixed experiences were described. Little information was identified about success of multi-agency work as perceived by young people. Some positive experiences with multi-agency working were also described. Barriers and facilitators of implementing multi-agency work: Barriers included insufficient resources, structural issues and policy and strategy limitations. Shared practices, priorities, and vision as well as information sharing between agencies were important. A significant practitioner-related factor was staff expertise. Barriers and facilitators of young people engaging with multi-agency work: Awareness of the services and flexibility in how young people could engage with them were important factors. More support with engagement for young people could act as a facilitator. Personal factors that might act as barriers to engagement for young people were identified. Good relationships with staff and positive staff attitudes were important facilitators. The review identified evidence gaps and a need for further robust research.

articles.title: Are carbon tariffs climate policy?
articles.authors: Gregory Casey, Kyle C. Meng, Ivan Rudik
articles.abstract: Carbon import tariffs, traditionally considered a complement to domestic climate policy, are increasingly proposed as standalone policies. We build a quantitative trade model to compare U.S. carbon tariffs with and without a domestic carbon tax, each applied to a set of carbon-intensive, trade-exposed sectors. We find three main results. First, a U.S. carbon tariff increases U.S. emissions, lowers foreign emissions, and on net achieves half the global emissions reductions of the combined policy, which lowers both U.S. and foreign emissions. Second, both approaches increase U.S. GDP and welfare, but the combined policy has a larger effect due to terms of trade improvements. Third, global emissions reductions from multilateral tariff-only agreements are modest and do not increase monotonically with greater membership, whereas under combined policies they scale considerably with membership.

articles.title: Smells Like Teen Rage Spirit: Anger Care and the Misrecognition of Emotional Control as Workplace Guidance
articles.abstract: This paper theorizes a workplace communication pattern in which anger-based guidance is morally framed by the actor as serious guidance, care for the subordinate, responsibility for the workplace, or necessary strictness, while recipients experience the same anger as immature rage, public scolding, rank display, humiliation, or emotional domination. I call this actor-side moral framing Anger Care: a communicative mode in which anger-based correction, instruction, or intervention is reconstructed as care, sincerity, responsibility, and legitimate guidance. This paper does not define Anger Care as inherently harmful. In some cases, anger may signal boundaries, seriousness, or the need for behavioral correction, particularly in response to dangerous acts, serious misconduct, or harm toward others. The problem arises when anger becomes dysregulated, publicly expressed, disproportionate, and damaging to recipients’ dignity, psychological safety, and ability to speak. I call this harmful form Dysregulated Anger Care. When it occurs in meetings, shared chats, public spaces, or messages visible to others, it becomes Public Dysregulated Anger Care. When anger is used to display the actor’s superior rank, authority, or dominance, it becomes Rank-Displaying Anger Care. By contrast, Smells Like Teen Rage Spirit captures the recipient-side, cynical perception of Anger Care. It describes the moment when a supervisor frames anger as “for the subordinate’s sake,” but the recipient senses something closer to immature emotional domination, public humiliation, or rank-based anger. In this sense, the phrase is not merely a parody, but a theoretical term for the recipient’s experiential detection of anger disguised as care. Drawing on research on anger, abusive supervision, workplace bullying, psychological safety, organizational silence, emotional labor, social learning, emotional contagion, memetics, and Structural Selection Pressure, this paper explains how Anger Care is produced, justified, perceived, and reproduced. It further connects Anger Care to Ma-Meme, Rage Authorization Reproduction, Rage Pattern Reproduction, Silent Harassment, and Structural Selection Pressure. The central argument is that workplace anger should not be evaluated only by whether anger occurred, but by how it is framed, where it is expressed, how it is experienced by recipients, and how it is reproduced within organizational culture.

articles.title: The Punk Rock Orchestra (PRO): A Single-Operator Methodology for the Critical Study of Human–Machine Communication Interfaces
articles.abstract: This paper introduces the Punk Rock Orchestra (PRO), a single-operator methodology for the critical study of human–machine communication interfaces, developed by an independent researcher without institutional affiliation. PRO addresses a structural gap in the study of LLM-based communicative systems: how to sustain rigorous, independent inquiry into the emergent properties of synthetic media interfaces under conditions of asymmetry between individual users and the corporate platforms that host them. The methodology operates through a multi-agent ensemble with explicit adversarial architecture, constructing synthetic specialized agents at three levels of cognitive density (N1/N2/N3) discriminated by five operational metrics, in order to interrogate the communicative behavior of contemporary large language model systems. The paper documents four primary contributions: (1) a three-tier taxonomy of agent specialization with preliminary cross-platform evidence of functional differentiation in synthetic media production; (2) a methodological framework based on the formal distinction between protocol-bound analysis (Robotic Class) and open synthesis (Dialogical Class), coordinated through Blue Team / Red Team / Forensic Layer triangulation, with the Sterling Protocol as architectural countermeasure to systemic sycophancy in synthetic communication; (3) the identification of context poisoning by provisioning failure — a previously uncharacterized communicative failure mode of high-density synthetic personas, taxonomically distinct from sycophancy, with proposed mitigation; and (4) a Pilot Study with 54 documented interactions across three commercial platforms (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini, DeepSeek) showing platform-dependent variation in the communicative resilience of synthetic agents under adversarial conditions. We position PRO as a critical methodology for studying the deliberative capacity of synthetic media interfaces, with explicit attention to the structural conditions under which independent communicative research can be conducted outside corporate platform infrastructure. The paper declares its limitations explicitly, including construct validity and methodological recursivity, treated as design parameters of independent media research rather than as concealed weaknesses.

articles.title: The Heart Slicer Model: Micro-Devaluation, Labeling, Silence, and the Reproduction of Harm in Workplace Communication
articles.abstract: This paper proposes the Heart Slicer Model as a theoretical framework for explaining how micro-devaluation, labeling, silence, public correction, avoidance, and comparison in workplace communication gradually position a target as less competent, less legitimate, or less included, thereby producing cumulative relational harm. Previous studies on workplace bullying, abusive supervision, workplace ostracism, organizational silence, and psychological safety have clarified how explicit aggression, exclusion, silence, and voice suppression affect individuals and organizations. However, workplace harm does not always appear as a single strong act such as verbal abuse, intimidation, insult, or explicit exclusion. In many cases, harm emerges through repeated low-intensity communicative acts, including minor corrections, public criticism, competence labels, hierarchical labels, non-response, exclusion from information sharing, and avoidant distancing. This paper conceptualizes such repeated low-intensity communicative acts as micro-devaluation and describes the cumulative process through which they erode the target’s self-understanding, voice possibility, action possibility, sense of belonging, and relational presence as heart slicing. Heart slicing is not merely psychological damage. It is a form of relational positioning in which the target is repeatedly treated as someone who is less capable, less central, less answerable, or less worthy of inclusion. The paper also theorizes secondary slicing, a process in which individuals who have been sliced reproduce similar communicative patterns toward weaker or less protected targets through public correction, labeling, silence, or avoidance. In this sense, heart slicing is not only an interpersonal event but also a communicative culture that may be learned, imitated, and reproduced within organizations. The Heart Slicer Model reframes workplace harm by asking not only what happened, but what was eroded through repetition. This perspective makes it possible to analyze invisible relational harm, silent exclusion, labeling-based positioning, shame produced by public correction, and the reproduction of harm through secondary slicing. By shifting attention from isolated events to cumulative communicative patterns, this paper provides a theoretical foundation for harassment prevention, managerial education, workplace consultation, and the redesign of organizational communication.

articles.title: Parliamentary Power in Early Representative Assemblies: Evidence from XVII century England
articles.abstract: The patterns of behaviour of representatives in early parliaments have not received much attention in the political science literature. In this article, we bring in new data and propose several theoretical mechanisms that can account for this shift in power towards the early legislative body. Using the case of the seventeenth-century English parliament and the records of its activities reported in the parliamentary journals as well as other sources of that period, we model and examine the policy-, vote- and office-seeking behaviour of England's seventeenth-century Parliament. We find that the primary changes in power, as measured by the number of legislative acts passed, can be attributed to the expansion of policy coverage. In contrast, vote-seeking and office-seeking behaviours appear to contribute less substantially to the expansion of parliamentary authority. Our findings have important implications for our understanding of how elites behave in early and contemporary representative assemblies.

articles.title: Monetary Policy Transmission and Equity Market Performance in Ghana: Nonlinear, Distributional, and Time-Frequency Evidence
articles.abstract: Monetary policy transmission to equity markets in frontier economies remains incompletely understood, particularly regarding nonlinear dynamics, distributional heterogeneity, and structural instability. This study investigates how monetary policy shocks affect equity market performance in Ghana — a frontier economy characterised by persistent macroeconomic volatility, shallow financial markets, and recurrent structural breaks — over the period January 2007 to December 2024 (N = 216 monthly observations). We employ a multi-method framework integrating Bayesian Quantile Regression (BQR), Toda–Yamamoto augmented VAR causality testing, impulse response functions (IRF), forecast error variance decomposition (FEVD), and continuous Morlet wavelet coherence analysis. Zivot–Andrews structural break unit root tests account for endogenous breaks associated with the 2008–2009 Global Financial Crisis, the 2014–2015 exchange rate crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic, and the 2022–2023 sovereign debt and inflation crisis. Monetary policy rate shocks are associated with statistically significant, asymmetric, and quantile-dependent negative effects on both the Ghana Stock Exchange Composite Index (GSE-CI) and the Financial Stock Index (GSE-FSI), with the largest contractionary effects concentrated at upper return quantiles. Toda–Yamamoto tests indicate unidirectional causality from monetary policy to equity returns. Wavelet coherence identifies medium-run co-movement (6–18 months) as the dominant transmission band, while FEVD estimates attribute approximately 21.4 percent of forecast error variance in GSE-CI returns to monetary policy innovations at a twelve-month horizon. These findings have practical relevance for investors managing interest rate risk, policymakers balancing disinflation against capital market development, and regulators designing macroprudential frameworks in frontier market contexts.

articles.title: Reliable Panel Regression: A Default Workﬂow for Slow-Moving, Mismeasured Variables
articles.abstract: Political scientists often interpret coeﬃcient shrinkage under ﬁxed eﬀects as evidence that pooled associations are confounded. This paper shows why that inference is unreliable for slow moving, mismeasured regressors. Fixed eﬀects can remove much of the signal and identify coeﬃcients from within unit variation that is disproportionately measurement error, attenuating estimates toward zero. A lone ﬁxed eﬀects coeﬃcient may therefore be unable to distinguish confounding from measurement error. I show that the attenuation depends on a regressor’s empirical intraclass correlation and measurement reliability. I then propose a default workﬂow for panel regression. Researchers estimate reliability when possible, report pooled and ﬁxed eﬀects estimates with corrected within reliability, use partial identiﬁcation bounds when the estimates share a sign, and report ﬁxed eﬀects as a within unit estimate when they do not. For variables with no reliability estimate, I introduce an autocorrelation frontier that bounds the attenuation factor directly. I conclude by applying this workﬂow to several published results to show that the data often cannot distinguish attenuation from confounding, and the workﬂow makes clear which case the researcher faces.

articles.title: Macroeconomic Effects of Climate Anomalies on Subnational Inflation in India: Heterogeneity, Monetary Policy and the Role of Inflation Targeting
articles.abstract: This paper provides novel subnational evidence on the inflationary effects of climate shocks in India using monthly data for 21 states during 2013–2023 and panel local projections. Four main findings emerge. First, temperature shocks persistently raise both headline and food inflation, with no evidence of mean reversion over the medium run. Second, these effects are heterogeneous, with stronger responses in rural areas, post-monsoon months, and agriculture-dependent states. Third, the response is asymmetric: warm anomalies drive most of the inflationary effect through agricultural supply disruptions, while cold anomalies remain statistically insignificant. Fourth, flexible inflation targeting weakens, but does not eliminate, climate induced inflationary pressures.

articles.title: “The Guardian is credible but leans towards opinion”: Shadow mechanisms in GPT-5’s web search and the politics of credibility
articles.authors: Petros Terzis, Kehan (Sky) Sheng, Marijn Sax, David Moats, Max van Drunen, Rebecca Sokol-Snyder, Ernesto de León, Asia Bega
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly replacing traditional search engines as people’s primary gateways to information. Yet, despite their growing significance and the increasingly central role of LLMs in political communication and participation, legal frameworks provide limited opportunities for transparency, let alone changes, over how politically sensitive prompts are handled. At the same time, scholarly debates about LLM objectivity remain fixated on statistical formulas for measuring political bias that presuppose a neutral ‘centre’ for contested issues (e.g. abortion), the deviation from which can be measured following traditional mental maps of bias analysis; an assumption, however, that lacks foundation in political and legal philosophy. This paper proposes an epistemic shift in how neutrality is framed and dealt with in LLM research and practice. First, we systematically critique the dominant ‘political bias’ paradigm, showing how scholars as well as government and industry actors frame neutrality in ways that entrench methodological limitations. Then, we conduct a multi-stage, mixed-methods pilot study to illuminate aspects of GPT-5’s web search that are core to the processes of information retrieval. To do so, we prompted GPT-5 API with a corpus of binary and politically loaded prompts focusing on the human rights stances of no table political figures (US, UK, EU). For each prompt, we retrieve all URLs (n= 22,500), search queries, and reasoning traces, paying close attention to the logic and parameters disclosed by GPT-5’s web search. After that, we deployed qualitative and quantitative methods to map how the model assesses the credibility of sources and to measure discrepancies between domain retrieval and citation frequencies, respectively. Departing from output-focused bias studies, our work reveals the infrastructural terrain where ‘LLM neutrality’ materialises. In particular, we: 1) identify misconceptions about how political bias is manifested and understood in practice; 2) pinpoint architectural details, operational gaps, and double standards in GPT-5’s web search functions; and 3) offer interdisciplinary recommendations for policymakers, practitioners, and researchers in the field.

articles.title: Football Empires: Simulating the Effects of Colonialism on the 2026 World Cup
articles.authors: Lucas Paulo da Silva, Thomas Schincariol, Morgan Wack
articles.abstract: European empires extracted vast resources from their colonies and continue to benefit from these legacies today. We examine an under-researched domain of this advantage: international football. Drawing on the literatures on colonial labor extraction, the "muscle drain" in global sports, and cumulative causation, we argue that colonial migration pipelines channel football talent from former colonies to former imperial powers, generating self-reinforcing competitive advantages. We test this argument using approximately 1,500 simulations of the (currently upcoming) FIFA World Cup 2026 tournament in Football Manager under three roster conditions: actual rosters, rosters with colony-background players redistributed to their ex-colonial "heritage nations," and rosters with colony-background players removed entirely. We identify 49 colony-background players across six European teams. All four pre-registered hypotheses find support. Former colonizer countries perform significantly worse without colony-background players and formerly colonized countries perform significantly better when they receive them. We also predict the World Cup 2026 results under each condition. France has the highest probability of winning the tournament with actual rosters in our simulations. However, it fields 17 colony-background players and sees its World Cup win rate nearly halve under redistribution of these players, while Brazil's prospects double. Former colonizer teams collectively account for over three-quarters of simulated tournament wins under actual rosters but lose more than 20 percentage points of that share when colony-background talent is redistributed. This study demonstrates that colonial legacies continue to shape competitive outcomes in international football, with consequences for the financial rewards, cultural prestige, and soft power that accompany World Cup success.

articles.title: Hidden Labor, Hidden Harm: Gendered Trauma Exposure in Community Supervision
articles.authors: Sandra L. Trappen, Ethan Galley, Cristina Figueroa, Bobur Rakhmatullaev
articles.abstract: This study examines how hierarchical structures organize the emotional and relational labor of federal probation officers, shaping the institutional conditions under which justice work occurs. Drawing on a national survey of 423 officers and in‑depth interviews with 30 participants, we show how expectations of composure, distributions of relational work, and organizational norms that discourage strain disclosure create gendered vulnerabilities across the workforce. Women, officers of color, and non‑supervisors report heightened demands and scrutiny, revealing how institutional cultures distribute emotional labor and constrain expressions of strain. Early adversity provides background context for how officers describe their work but does not function as an explanatory mechanism. Qualitative accounts illuminate the paradox of double exposure: officers are both survivors of trauma and practitioners managing others’ trauma within environments that reward stoicism and limit vulnerability. Strain emerges as the operational expression of structurally produced trauma, underscoring that trauma‑informed justice must begin with the workforce.

articles.title: When Does AI Pay for Itself? A Cost-Benefit Analysis of AI-Assisted Police Report Writing
articles.abstract: Police departments across the US are rapidly adopting AI-assisted report-writing, yet its cost-benefit case remaines largely unexamined, and the field-trial evidence can be read as either favorable or unfavorable. This paper presents a probabilistic cost-benefit analysis of one such deployment at the Manchester Police Department, varying how the tool's cost is attributed and how much of the measured productivity gain is credited to the AI rather than to organizational learning. Under per-officer subscription pricing, the tool pays for itself. For a medium department, the average net present value is $2.3 million under a favorable, frontier-based reading of the productivity evidence and $0.8 million under a conservative reading anchored to the null result of the department's randomized controlled trial, with a positive return likely in both. Attributing a bundled vendor contract to the program instead yields a negative return. Cost attribution, therefore, determines the sign of the result, while the extent to which the gain is credited to the tool governs how confident a department can be in adopting this technology.

articles.title: The Welfare State in Egypt, 1995-2005: A Comparative Approach
articles.abstract: This article applies Esping-Andersen's welfare state typology to Egypt from 1995 to 2005. It examines state, market, and society relations during market-oriented reforms and analyzes seven welfare schemes and safety nets. It characterizes Egypt's welfare state as conservative/informal, with benefits tied to formal employment and families, religious institutions, and clientelistic networks filling gaps for the informal sector.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Trust Nobody: How Voters React To Conspiracy Theories
articles.authors: Giovanna Maria Invernizzi, Ahmed Ezzeldin Mohamed
articles.abstract: Using an online experiment among US subjects, this paper shows that exposure to conspiracy theories decreases trust in political institutions and information providers. The conspiracy theories were unrelated to American domestic politics, underscoring the broader danger of such narratives. The paper finds that voters do not appear to weigh unrelated conspiracies when evaluating politicians' performance.

articles.title: Religious Cycles of Government Responsiveness: Why Governments Distribute in Ramadan
articles.abstract: This paper studies why governments in Muslim autocracies expand distributive policies and service delivery during Ramadan. Focusing on Egypt from 2014 to 2020, it argues that autocratic regimes distribute during Ramadan to contain political threats emerging from reputational pressure and mobilization risks. Using municipal-level data on government-reported economic benefits, it finds greater reported distribution in Ramadan, especially in places where threats are higher.

articles.title: The Relationship between State-Provided Islamic Education and Islamism
articles.abstract: This article examines the relationship between state-provided religious education and support for Islamists in Egypt. It finds that recipients of state-provided Islamic education, Azharites, are more likely to hold favorable views of Islamists, likely because of ideological alignment around religion's public role and conservative social norms. The analysis does not support the idea that Azharites view Islamists as competitors in the religious market.

articles.title: Toward Human-Centered Sustainability. Emotional, Relational, and Cultural Utility in Economic Behavior: Insights from Thailand
articles.abstract: Contemporary sustainability debates increasingly recognize that ecological crises cannot be understood solely through assumptions of rational utility maximization and growth-centered development. Drawing on insights from behavioral economics, well-being research, and sustainability studies, the paper develops a broader conception of utility that incorporates emotional, relational, and cultural dimensions of human welfare. It makes three contributions. First, it proposes emotional utility, relational utility, and cultural utility as complementary dimensions of human welfare that help explain why well-being depends upon psychological stability, social relationships, and culturally meaningful forms of life. Second, it introduces the concept of behavioral sustainability, defined as the long-term reproduction of sustainable patterns of behavior through supportive social, emotional, cultural, and institutional conditions. Third, it illustrates the relevance of this framework through the case of Thailand, where concepts such as sabai-sabai, kreng jai, nam jai, and the Sufficiency Economy Philosophy reflect culturally grounded understandings of well-being that go beyond material accumulation. The analysis suggests that sustainability should be evaluated not only according to ecological outcomes and economic performance but also according to its capacity to support trust, belonging, resilience, and enduring quality of life. Sustainable development therefore requires conceptions of welfare that account for what people need to live well, not only what they consume.

articles.title: Overcoming construction constraints for the supply of new detached and high-rise housing
articles.authors: Megan Robinson, Andrea Sharam, Ali Zolghadr, Louise Dorignon, Ron Wakefield, Ehsan Gharaie, Mehrdad Arashpour, Peter Fairbrother, Anil Sawhney, Mohammed Mojtahedi
articles.abstract: This Inquiry provides the first significant examination of the Australian construction sector’s ability to deliver both detached and high-rise housing. It analyses construction workflows, markets, regulation, workforce, technologies and supply chains. The housing construction industry in Australia is at crisis point. The rate of Australia’s detached housing output has been largely static since at least 1980. Apartment construction can lift in response to demand, but output is volatile. There is no overarching strategy aimed at addressing housing construction constraints. This Inquiry provides eight interconnected options for policymakers and industry.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Networked Territorial Resilience
articles.abstract: In this paper, I build a middle-range theory of territorial resilience. The core argument is straightforward yet frequently overlooked: territorial prosperity and long-term adaptive capacity emerge not from how we pump material and financial resources into a region, but from how we distribute its solution-generation capacity. In the context of polycrisis – intersecting climate, geopolitical, economic, demographic, and institutional shocks – conventional development paradigms centered on spatial and institutional concentration create systemic fragility through single points of failure and limited adaptability. The framework introduces Territorial Cell Theory, which conceptualizes territorial units (ranging from municipalities and hromadas to cities, regions, and autonomies) as functionally defined adaptive socio-ecological systems.
