This theory is operationalized through the Hive Governance Model – a polycentric, multi-layered architecture – supported by the Development Capacity Engine (comprising Agency, Knowledge, Trust, and Experimentation), Territorial Solution Generation Capacity (TSGC) as a dynamic measure of adaptive flow, the Territorial Resilience Index (TRI) as a composite measure of resilience stock, and the Network Intelligence Principle that governs collective learning and diffusion of successful practices. The Territorial Evolution Cycle describes the iterative process of observation, experimentation, adaptation, replication, and network-level evolution. Drawing on practice-informed comparative illustrations from South Tyrol (a mature European autonomy), Ukraine (a wartime stress-test of decentralization), and Central Asia (resource-constrained traditional community systems), the analysis demonstrates the framework’s cross-scale and context-adaptive applicability. The findings suggest that policy investments oriented toward strengthening distributed solution-generation mechanisms may yield superior long-term resilience and sustainability outcomes compared to traditional resource-concentration strategies.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Beyond Humanitarian and Securitization Paradigms. Cultural mediation, local governance, and the missing layer in European migration integration policies
articles.abstract: European migration governance remains largely deadlocked between two dominant frameworks: rights-based humanitarian protection and state-led securitization. While both address critical vectors – namely baseline legal admission and public order – they systematically bypass the post-arrival phase, where long-term integration dynamics are actually negotiated through daily contact between newcomers, host populations, and municipal state organs. This paper argues that chronic integration failures stem primarily from structural deficiencies in this post-arrival architecture, specifically the lack of institutionalized, professional socio-cultural mediation. We introduce the concept of localized social stabilization mechanisms to capture community-level adjustments occurring when residents perceive formal institutions as incapable of managing rapid demographic shifts, maintaining normative predictability, or enforcing civic reciprocity. Rather than treating these grassroots frictions as mere expressions of xenophobia, our framework approaches them as complex sociological indicators of institutional trust decay. Using a constructivist-institutionalist lens, the paper outlines a five-dimensional integration matrix: legal admission, socio-economic participation, cultural adaptation, civic reciprocity, and local trust-building. Our central contribution is the conceptualization of a “missing layer” – a professionalized, locally deployed mediation infrastructure – as a structural prerequisite for sustainable integration. Ultimately, the analysis demonstrates how strengthening this relational layer allows municipal states to transition from reactive crisis management to adaptive governance models capable of balancing social cohesion with broader European human rights obligations. At the same time, the framework stresses the asymmetrical nature of adaptation: cultural mediation should primarily facilitate newcomers’ acquisition of core host society norms (rule of law, gender equality, secular public space, and individual rights) rather than open-ended mutual compromise.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Value Contamination in Policy Simulation: Measuring How LLM Policy-Value Orientations Shape Predicted Administrative Burden Effects
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate behavioral re-sponses to policy interventions, but their simulated treatment effects may depend on latent policy-value assumptions. This paper develops an audit framework for measuring value sensitivity in LLM-generated policy simulations. Using adminis-trative burden in vaccination access as a testbed, we evaluate fifteen LLM endpoints spanning nine provider or model-family ecosystems. Each model completes a policy- value orientation battery and then predicts vaccination willingness for 27 synthetic profiles under low- and high-burden access conditions. The results do not support a simple composite-contamination account. A com- posite policy-value contamination (PVC) index does not predict cross-model vari- ation in administrative-burden effects, even in the expanded fifteen-model sample. Dimension-specific checks show that an initial collective-obligation signal is not stable after expanding the model sample, underscoring the limits of small model- level regressions. Narrative frame-decomposition provides more robust evidence: access-barrier and coercive-backlash rationales are associated with larger burden effects, while collective-responsibility rationales are associated with smaller effects. The strongest evidence comes from a within-model frame-manipulation experiment conducted on a four-model subset: autonomy and equity/access frames increase predicted burden effects, whereas collective-obligation frames reduce them. The findings show that LLM policy simulations should not be treated as neutral behavioral forecasts, but neither are they well described by a single composite ide-ological score. Value sensitivity appears through expressed interpretive frames and prompt-induced governance lenses more clearly than through an aggregate cross- model orientation index. The paper contributes a framework for auditing LLM- based policy simulations by linking measured value orientations, simulated treat- ment effects, narrative mechanisms, and within-model frame responsiveness.

articles.title: When technology levels: the cost of arms and wealth inequality in the ancient world
articles.abstract: A long tradition in historical sociology holds that broad military participation makes societies more egalitarian, but the idea is hard to test. This paper uses a global archaeological database of settlement-level wealth Gini coefficients (Bogaard et al. 2025) to test a version of the Andreski (1954) hypothesis: that iron, a cheap, abundant, and mass-producible metal, lowered inequality by broadening who could be armed, while bronze, an expensive and elite-controlled metal alloy, favored the concentration of wealth by keeping the means of violence tightly controlled. In the Eurasian ancient world, where both metals were indigenous, three econometric designs support the “democratization of violence” thesis. First, within the same locality and holding the appropriability of agricultural production constant, iron is associated with a Gini of wealth about 0.07–0.10 lower, while bronze carries no association. Second, an event study around iron’s local adoption shows a similar effect. Third, in a spectrum of technologies, iron is the only one that reduces inequality: bronze, the plow and animal traction, cavalry, and writing are all null, as the cost structure of technology predicts. The estimates are robust to plausible error in the coded adoption dates. The findings are exploratory and concentrated in the Iron Age Mediterranean and Britain.

articles.title: From Crisology to Polycrisis: A Genealogy of the Concept of Polycrisis
articles.abstract: Since 2022, "polycrisis" has become a global category of analysis (ECB, IMF, UN, COP climate conferences, Davos, public-policy think tanks), yet this institutional success has been accompanied by an almost total effacement of its theoretical source. This article reconstructs fifty years of that genealogy (1976-2026). Edgar Morin laid the foundations as early as 1976 in "Pour une crisologie" (a return to the Greek krisis, a reflexive problematization of the "crisis of the concept of crisis"). The term and concept appear in 1993 in Terre-Patrie, are reformulated in 2011 (La Voie) and extended in 2014 (Enseigner à vivre). Method. Three cross-referenced corpora: four founding Morinian texts; 257 academic texts (1993-2024) manually coded on 51 variables; a multilingual bibliometric survey across five civilizational areas and fifteen linguistic variants. Triangulation across three sources of different natures is required. The coded corpus will be deposited as open supplementary data. Four results. (1) Explicit citation of Morin falls from 100% in 1993-2006 to 6% in 2022-2024; Google Scholar mentions rise from fewer than 100 to more than 7,600 annual occurrences between 2020 and 2025; the share of English rises from 40% to 83%. (2) Three multilingual anteriorities: "policrisis" in Spanish in Buenos Aires as early as 1993 (29 years before Davos-Tooze); two Chinese calques by Ma Shengli in 1997 (16 years before the Japanese use); continuous Hispanophone academic diffusion since 1995. (3) The Bakhtin-Morin filiation is philologically refuted: only three occurrences across 19 cardinal works, all in non-conceptual contexts; the lineage is named by Morin himself (1999 Chinese preface) without Bakhtin. (4) Three translational shifts in Homeland Earth (Hampton Press, 1999) contribute to the concept's invisibility in the pre-2022 Anglophone reception. Four analytical contributions: second-order crisis; a four-register typological grid (R1-R4); qualitative substitution characterizing the post-2022 paradigmatic relocation; a mapping in six contemporary prisms of polycrisis. Results are ranked across three levels of proof. The stance is explicitly claimed as a regulative ideal in the Kantian sense. Four external philological verifications remain open without affecting the main results. Résumé en français. Cet article retrace cinquante ans de généalogie du concept de polycrise (1976-2026). À partir de trois corpus triangulés couvrant quinze langues, il documente l'érosion de la citation explicite de Morin (100 % à 6 %), trois antériorités multilingues (espagnol 1993, chinois 1997, japonais 2013), l'infirmation philologique de la prétendue filiation Bakhtine-Morin, et trois glissements traductifs dans Homeland Earth (1999). Quatre outils analytiques sont proposés. Bilingual edition. French version of record: De la crisologie à polycrisis : Généalogie du concept de polycrise (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20369644). English version: DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20539381.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Concrete Interests: Paramilitary City-Building in Rio de Janeiro
articles.abstract: How do armed groups reshape cities? This article argues that Rio de Janeiro’s milícias—paramilitary organizations, frequently led by current and former security officers,—govern territory through “build-to-hold” urbanization. Rather than simply extracting rents, milícias construct informal housing to expand captive populations that generate recurring income, electoral leverage, and political protection. Combining geospatial data on territorial control with satellite-derived measures of urban development from 2016–2023, I use staggered difference-in-differences estimators to show that milícia entry causes sustained increases in building density and height. These effects are absent in gang-controlled areas and persist in territories transitioning from gangs to milícias, indicating that densification is specific to the milícia governance model. I further show that increased construction does not increase formal housing prices or transaction activity, consistent with a system in which armed actors retain control over housing assets rather than integrating them into formal markets. The findings demonstrate how criminal organizations actively produce urban space in ways that entrench coercive authority and blur the boundary between criminal governance and state power.

articles.title: BANGLADESHI MEDITERRANEAN Rethinking Arrival in a Post-Postcolonial Migration Corridor
articles.authors: Nasibul Hoque, Mohammad Hossain Bin Idrish, Md Masud Rana
articles.abstract: Over the past decade, Bangladeshi nationals have consistently ranked among the top 10 nationalities, and since 2024, they have become the leading group crossing the deadly Central Mediterranean Route (CMR) to Italy. This statistical regularity is often ignored by current frameworks. Bangladesh has no colonial ties to Italy, is located 9,000 kilometers from Sicily, and mainly hosts Forcibly Displaced Rohingya people rather than producing them. In 2025, Bangladesh accounted for 30.5 percent of all arrivals through the Central Mediterranean, a share no other non-colonial, non-proximate nationalities have matched in the corridor’s history. This paper argues that the Bangladeshi case challenges the dominant postcolonial perspective in Mediterranean migration studies and highlights a notable gap in that framework. Applying a four-pillar analytical model: Lefebvre’s (1991) idea of produced space, Massey’s (1993, 2005) power geometry, Duncan and Ley’s (1993) moral landscape, and Carling’s (2002, 2018) aspiration-capability, the paper introduces the concept of Post-postcolonial Migration Corridor. This reconceptualizes the migration corridor as a transnational spatial formation shaped by recruitment networks and migrant imaginaries, rather than as a remnant of colonial administration and geographic proximity. Using UNHCR arrival data (2016–2026), IOM mortality figures for descriptive statistical analysis, and structured qualitative synthesis, the paper makes a comparative argument no single-corridor study has yet made: Bangladesh is the only non-colonial, non-proximate corridor to hold a top-five ranking in post-2020 years. Finally, the resulting analytical category, the post-postcolonial Migration corridor, does not represent a complete break from the existing post-colonial scholarship; rather, it provides an additional perspective that the dominant framework should incorporate.

articles.title: Democratic Accountability with Citizen Coproduction
articles.abstract: Many policies require citizens to actively participate in their implementation in order to be successful, a situation referred to as citizen coproduction. Though citizen coproduction is necessary in many policy areas, it could create difficulties for democratic accountability because citizens and politicians share responsibility for policy failures and attribution of blame may be difficult. We analyze the problem of democratic accountability with citizen coproduction in the context of a model in which politicians, who vary with respect to their competence at selecting policies, choose whether to implement policies whose success depends on citizen coproduction choices. For citizens, the problem is one of attribution: when coproduction is low, it may be hard to select good incumbents because policy failures are frequent even when policy choices are good. Incumbents anticipate these effects, which sometimes implies that they are less likely to choose reforms when they are more likely to be successful.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: From Recognition to Repair: Increasing Support for Reconciliation in a Settler Colonial Society
articles.authors: Andreea Zota, Marco M. Aviña, Jo-Anne Wemmers
articles.abstract: Historical injustice against marginalized groups creates a persistent democratic challenge: publics may acknowledge harm while resisting reparative measures. Can communication strategies bridge this gap? We study this question in the context of reconciliation with Indigenous Peoples in Canada. In a preregistered survey experiment with 3,017 non-Indigenous adults, we compare statistical and narrative interventions conveying parallel information about contemporary inequalities linked to past harms. We find that symbolic recognition is substantially more popular than materially, politically, or behaviorally demanding forms of repair. Statistical information is especially effective for recognition and advocacy, whereas narratives more strongly reduce resentment toward Indigenous Peoples and increase support for reparative policies. Combining formats produces diminishing returns, suggesting that more information is not always more persuasive. Focus groups help explain why: statistics make inequality legible but abstract, whereas narratives make harm concrete without automatically translating emotion into support for more demanding repair. Together, the findings show that recognition and repair are distinct dimensions of reconciliation and that different communication strategies are effective for each.

articles.title: Moral Adaptation: Sustaining Climate Activism When Futures Fail
articles.abstract: How does collective action persist when the futures that once motivated it become implausible? Existing scholarship highlights moral commitments and expectation management, yet it typically treats these dimensions in parallel. Based on a two-year longitudinal study of a German climate resistance group, this paper argues that moral grammars are temporally structured and subject to reorganization. As doubts about the preventability of climate catastrophe grew, the group engaged in moral adaptation: shifting from a consequentialist orientation anchored in catastrophe prevention toward a virtue-ethical orientation centered on responsible conduct under conditions of crisis. Unlike temporal work, which preserves future horizons, moral adaptation relocated moral evaluation away from anticipated outcomes and toward present conduct, redefining the meanings of strategy, obligation, and community, and preceding the group’s later organizational transformation. The paper theorizes moral adaptation as a mechanism of resilience distinct from identity maintenance, emotional management, and temporal work. In doing so, it advances the case for treating moral grammars as dynamic, evaluative structures whose temporal orientation can shift under adverse conditions, contributing to the integration of the sociology of morality and the sociology of temporality.

articles.title: Factors associated with disclosure of protected characteristics: a systematic review and meta-synthesis
articles.authors: George Wood, Rachel Connolly, David Nicholls, Jeremy Dietz
articles.abstract: Background: Employers increasingly collect equality, diversity and inclusion data, and reducing non-disclosure rates is often used as a key performance indicator. However, why people with protected characteristics choose either to disclose or withhold this information at work has not, to our knowledge, been systematically reviewed. This review is a thematic synthesis of qualitative studies examining the experiences of disclosure in the workplace of people with protected characteristics according to UK law including disability, gender reassignment, race and sexual orientation. Methods: A grey literature search was conducted in October 2022 using Google, Lens.org and Web of Science to identify English-language systematic reviews and qualitative studies on adults’ workplace experiences of disclosing or not disclosing protected characteristics. A thematic synthesis was developed from these studies, and the search was rerun in July 2025 to identify additional evidence and update the themes. The risk of bias of each study was assessed using the CASP qualitative checklist. Results: Findings from 60 papers were included in the review, including two meta-ethnographies and a further 19 studies. The thematic synthesis yielded five main themes from the experiences and views on disclosure in the workplace from adults with protected characteristics: 1) Disclosure is an important, ongoing life event that has significant implications; 2) When people do not disclose, they may develop strategies to moderate disclosure that require energy to maintain; 3) Moderators of disclosure; 4) Positive consequences of disclosure; 5) Negative consequences of disclosure. Limitations: There was limited evidence on the experience of people with visible disabilities, bisexual people, and trans people, and no evidence discussing the experiences of people disclosing information about ethnicity. No studies were found discussing intersectionality of identities and how this might affect disclosure. Limited information was reported regarding participants' jobs, and some of the studies were older and conducted in contexts that might be less likely to reflect UK culture today. Conclusions: Employers who focus on creating a trusting, supportive environment; actively mitigating the risks of disclosure; reassuring staff about how disclosures will be handled; and meeting accessibility and adjustment needs can improve the workplace experiences of people with minority identities and potentially increase disclosure rates.

articles.title: Real Estate Wealth Is Not a Monolith: Non-Primary Real Estate and Its Role in Wealth Concentration
articles.authors: Pietro Valetto, Stefano Filauro, Ive Marx, Janet C. Gornick, Carolyn Fisher
articles.abstract: Housing wealth is widely recognized as the most equally distributed major asset class in advanced economies, anchoring middle-class wealth portfolios and overall wealth inequality. Yet, in this paper we observe how treating real estate as a monolith obscures a critical divide. Disaggregating net housing wealth into primary real estate equity and non-primary real estate equity reveals different distributional patterns that fundamentally alter our understanding of housing's role in the wealth distribution. Using harmonized microdata from the EU HFCS (2021) and U.S. SCF (2022), we show that while primary residences are indeed more equally distributed than overall wealth, non-primary real estate such as vacation homes, rental properties, land, and business properties makes up the most highly concentrated component of household portfolios, surpassing even financial assets. Our Gini factor decomposition analysis across various European countries and the U.S. reveals that non-primary real estate exhibits extreme concentration, with Gini coefficients ranging from 0.88 to 0.96, and contributes disproportionately to overall inequality relative to its wealth share. Therefore, the equalizing effect of housing relative to other asset classes emphasized in prior research holds only for owner-occupied homes. These findings suggest that policies treating all housing uniformly may inadvertently amplify wealth concentration, particularly in Europe where non-primary property ownership is nearly twice as prevalent as in the United States. (Stone Center Working Paper Series)

articles.title: Can Nonprofits Survive their Founders? Assessing Succession Effects in Private Museums
articles.abstract: Executive succession has widely been seen as disruptive for nonprofit organizations, negatively affecting performance in general and survival in particular. However, quantitative research on the succession-survival link in nonprofit organizations is limited, and findings are mixed. To extend knowledge in this area, a study on succession effects is conducted for a population of private art museums. These arts organizations show heavy founder involvement in funding, governance and vision, and have hence been argued to be vulnerable to executive succession. Succession effects in event-history analysis (368 organizations, 4371 organization-years) are only moderate and insignificant, yet might be more detrimental in some cases, such as for organizations that are small and opened by male founders. It is argued that considering the contingencies of organizational succession is required to comprehensively assess its hazards and opportunities.

articles.title: Stalled or distorted? Tempo Effects in Africa’s Fertility Transition
articles.abstract: Fertility rates in Africa have remained high, declined slowly, and stalled before reaching replacement level. However, there is little consensus on which countries truly stalled as existing research uses a variety of competing methods that are disconnected from fertility theory, do not account for tempo effects in the Total Fertility Rate, and rely too heavily on subjective researcher judgment. This study revisits fertility stalls using the first and second derivatives of tempo-adjusted TFR and a piecewise regression approach. This method reveals four previously unidentified stalls in Sudan, Botswana, Mauritania, and South Africa, as well as nine further stalls in other countries. Stalls in Botswana, South Africa, and Zimbabwe are particularly severe. While tempo effects do not fully explain stalls, they are positively associated, suggesting that stalls may partially reflect changes in the timing rather than total amount of fertility.

articles.title: Sanctioned Scholars: The Price Of Speaking Freely In Today’s Academy
articles.abstract: The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), a nonprofit organization committed to defending and sustaining the individual rights of all Americans to free speech and free thought, maintains a database documenting the ways and reasons scholars have faced calls for sanction because of their speech. This report presents the results of a survey of 209 scholars in this Scholars Under Fire database who were targeted for sanction because of their speech between 2020 and 2024.

articles.title: Relational Wealth: Ownership Diffusion and Ownership Confusion in Wealthy Families
articles.abstract: Economic elites have accumulated staggering resources. But scholars tend to conceptualize their wealth as singularly owned by individuals or households, neglecting long-established relational aspects of economic behavior. Using 30 interviews and six months of in-depth ethnographic observation at a “family office” that manages large fortunes, I ask how relationships shape elite perceptions of wealth ownership and the subsequent flows of family wealth. I find that when assets are legally shared (“diffused”), ownership perceptions can mismatch, and ownership can be “confused”. When owner- ship is confused, diffusing actors like family offices can influence ownership perceptions, shaping how elites wield wealth in practice. These shifts usually, but not exclusively, benefit “patriarchs.” Understanding the increasingly consequential resources of economic elites requires us to examine the assumption of unambiguous ownership: elite wealth is fundamentally entangled in familial and professional relationships.

articles.title: ROI and the Misclassification of Criminal Justice Education Programs: How Accountability Metrics Shape Public Universities and the Evaluation of CJ Education
articles.abstract: Criminal Justice (CJ) education programs at public universities are increasingly evaluated through state level ROI frameworks that tie program value to short term earnings and workforce alignment. This article shows how these models, now embedded in many funding and accountability regimes, tend to misclassify CJ education programs. Descriptive data from the National Center for O*NET Development, BLS, College Scorecard, and state salary databases reveal three key structural mismatches: most CJ occupations do not require a bachelor’s degree; public sector wage structures depress early career earnings; and ROI classifications shift with occupational and temporal definitions. As a result, ROI models capture labor market composition and state funding priorities rather than program value, obscuring CJ’s civic and community roles.

articles.title: CONVERGENT FAULTS : A Quantitative Forensic of Private Credit's Synchronized Systemic Risk (2026–2027)
articles.abstract: Private credit has gone from roughly $400 billion in 2009 to about $3 trillion in mid-2026. U.S. business development companies (BDCs) now hold close to half a trillion of that, split across three architectures with very different liquidity and disclosure properties. The official line, repeated by the Federal Reserve, the Financial Stability Board and most sell-side credit desks, calls the resulting risk "limited and manageable." Fund-level leverage is low, defaults are below the long-run leveraged-loan average, and bank exposure looks bounded. We argue that the official line works only when each stress channel is examined alone, and breaks down once the channels are read together. This paper documents five channels: software and SaaS valuation compression under AI disruption; bank-to-non-bank credit tightening, with JPMorgan as first mover; retail liquidity gating at perpetual non-traded BDCs; the "Bermuda Triangle" of related-party lending between Apollo and Athene, KKR and Global Atlantic, Brookfield and its affiliated insurers; and the 2026–2027 refinancing wall hitting a sector that has just lost its bid. Using Bloomberg Intelligence proprietary research, regulatory data and the public filings of the largest BDCs, we show that these channels are coupled. Each one tightens the others under plausible macroeconomic conditions, and the joint stress horizon is short enough to read on a calendar. The cascade is already visible. On 4 June 2026 Blackstone Private Credit Fund, the largest BDC, $82.1 billion of holdings, gated quarterly redemptions at 5% of NAV against requests of 10%. Its $1 billion 6% 2032 notes trade 150 basis points wide of triple-B financial peers, against an adviser (Blackstone Inc.) rated A+/A+. We map the catalysts that lie ahead, assign probabilities to three outcome scenarios, and identify a convergence window centred on Q1–Q2 2027.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Broad Support for Democratic Erosion: The Case of Trump’s TSA Order
articles.abstract: Public opinion surveys consistently find high support for democracy in the abstract. Studying the case of a popular but unconstitutional executive order, this note shows that broad, cross-partisan support for democratic erosion can emerge when it is attached to a popular policy goal. In March 2026, U.S. president Donald Trump ordered government to pay Transportation Security Administration (TSA) agents despite the lack of a congressional appropriation, in plain violation of Article I, Section 9 of the U.S. Constitution. About two-thirds of Americans approved of the order, including 59% of Democrats and 2024 Kamala Harris voters. Approval was substantially higher among those who correctly answered a question about which branch of government decides how money is spent under the Constitution. The findings suggest that episodes of executive aggrandizement can enjoy broad support when they are attached to popular policy goals, and that civic knowledge does not necessarily act as a bulwark against such support.

articles.title: Ethically Informed Algorithmic Matching and Refugee Resettlement
articles.authors: Ahmed Ezzeldin Mohamed, Craig Damian Smith
articles.abstract: This article discusses real-world projects using algorithms to match resettled refugees with sponsors and services. It argues that ethically informed algorithmic matching can support larger-scale and better-informed resettlement while avoiding simplistic techno-sceptical or techno-optimistic framings. The article emphasizes the dual-use function of technology in international mobility and the importance of ethical design.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Fathers and menstruation: a scoping review
articles.authors: Lavanya Gera, Danielle Jane Russell, Dani Barrington
articles.abstract: Introduction: Despite the well-established influence of family environments on menstrual health, existing research overwhelmingly centres mothers, leaving fathers’ contributions insufficiently understood. Yet Fathers have the potential to play an influential role in their daughters’ menstrual experiences. This review synthesises qualitative evidence on paternal involvement globally, using Lamb’s triadic father involvement framework (accessibility, engagement, and responsibility) to understand how fathers participate in, respond to, or are excluded from menstrual related interactions. Methods: A scoping review of qualitative or qualitative components of mixed-methods peer-reviewed studies published after 2000 was conducted. Studies were compiled through a four-stage search strategy followed by screening. Thematic synthesis was achieved through deductive coding using Lamb’s framework and inductive identification of contextual influences. Results: 105 studies met the inclusion criteria. Fathers demonstrated both positive and negative accessibility and engagement, particularly in relation to provision of menstrual products and willingness to discuss menstrual issues. Positive responsibility was evident in facilitating healthcare, though findings were limited. Three inductive themes; menstrual concealment, perceived lack of credibility, and maternal gatekeeping shaped father involvement across the three domains, often undermining fathers’ willingness and capacity to support daughters, despite expressing concern about their daughters’ menstrual experience. Conclusion: Fathers’ involvement in menstrual experiences is shaped by paternal knowledge gaps regarding menstruation, gendered norms, and family dynamics that promote menstrual concealment. Evidence remains fragmented and rarely explores the drivers of paternal behaviour. Findings highlight opportunities for father-inclusive education, stigma-reduction, and structural change, and underscore the need for research across diverse cultural contexts.

articles.title: El ciclo de las Políticas Sociales
articles.abstract: Documento de carácter académico y docente que analiza el ciclo de vida de las políticas sociales como marco para comprender cómo se construyen, aplican, evalúan y reformulan las respuestas públicas ante los problemas sociales. El texto presenta las cuatro etapas principales del ciclo —diagnóstico, diseño, implementación y evaluación—, explicando sus funciones, elementos clave y ejemplos aplicados al contexto español. Asimismo, se reflexiona sobre factores transversales que condicionan todo el proceso, como la relación entre agenda pública y agenda gubernamental, la participación social, la sostenibilidad institucional y el equilibrio entre estandarización y flexibilidad. Se trata, en suma, de un material de síntesis orientado a la comprensión teórica y a la práctica profesional.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: LA FORMACIÓN CRÍTICA DEL PSICÓLOGO. DERECHOS HUMANOS Y ÉTICA PROFESIONAL.
articles.abstract: Este trabajo propone reflexionar críticamente acerca del rol de la Universidad, en relación a su principal deber y función consagrado en la formación académica. Se analiza como problemática actual el déficit o ausencia de ciertos contenidos claves en la formación universitaria, particularmente del psicólogo/a. Vinculando dicha problemática al cuestionamiento de la obsolescencia del proceso educativo universitario. Repensando dicho proceso como una práctica compleja y dialéctica, que debe ser abordada de forma tal que responda a las necesidades de la sociedad. Partiendo desde la perspectiva de autonomía en el proceso de aprendizaje activo, entendido como modelo deseable. En función de la eficacia e impacto social de la Universidad, se expone la creciente necesidad del tratamiento explícito, obligatorio y constante de la cuestión Derechos Humanos y Ética. Entendido como contenido curricular que no puede faltar en la formación de un profesional crítico, capacitado y comprometido con la sociedad y sus problemáticas actuales. Si bien gran parte del análisis se centra en la Facultad de psicología de la UNMDP, se entiende que el debate excede la especificidad de nuestra carrera y puede extenderse a otras carreras de acuerdo a sus particularidades y singularidades. This work proposes to critically reflect on the role of the University regarding its main duty and function enshrined in academic training. The deficit or absence of certain key contents in university education, particularly for psychologists, is analyzed as a current problem. This issue is linked to questioning the obsolescence of the university educational process, rethinking said process as a complex and dialectical practice that must be approached in such a way that it responds to the needs of society. Starting from the perspective of autonomy in the active learning process, understood as a desirable model, and based on the efficacy and social impact of the University, the growing need for the explicit, mandatory, and constant treatment of Human Rights and Ethics is presented. This is understood as a curricular content that cannot be missing from the training of a critical, qualified professional committed to society and its current problems. Although a large part of the analysis focuses on the Faculty of Psychology at the UNMDP, it is understood that the debate exceeds the specificity of our program and can be extended to other programs according to their particularities and singularities.

articles.title: LA FANTASÍA EN LA PRÁCTICA EDUCATIVA COMO OBSTÁCULO EN LA FORMACIÓN CRÍTICA DEL PSICÓLOGO. DERECHOS HUMANOS, VALORES Y ÉTICA.
articles.abstract: Este trabajo presenta un análisis crítico sobre la formación profesional del psicólogo en la universidad pública, poniendo el foco en las prácticas pedagógicas y la necesidad de una inclusión transversal de los Derechos Humanos, los Valores y la Ética profesional. A partir de una mirada transdisciplinar que articula la Pedagogía Social y la vivencia institucional en la Facultad de Psicología (UNMdP), se conceptualiza el fenómeno de "la fantasía en la práctica educativa". Este concepto busca cuestionar las prácticas actuales educativas como un dilema estructural, presenta la contradicción existente entre un discurso oficial de horizontalidad y prácticas concretas escolarizantes, pasivas y verticalistas que alienan al estudiante y reproducen la desigualdad social. Finalmente, fundamentado en la Pedagogía de la Pregunta de Paulo Freire y en el marco de la Ley de Educación Superior (Art. 43) y la Res. Ministerial 343/09, se propone desnaturalizar las lógicas universitarias actuales para transitar de una mera transmisión de conocimientos hacia una praxis comprometida, orientada a la producción de profesionales críticos y hacedores capaces de responder a las demandas sociales actuales y promover una sociedad más justa. This work presents a critical analysis of the professional training of psychologists in public universities, focusing on pedagogical practices and the need for a transversal inclusion of Human Rights, Values, and Professional Ethics. From a transdisciplinary perspective that articulates Social Pedagogy and institutional experience at the Faculty of Psychology (UNMdP), the phenomenon of "fantasy in educational practice" is conceptualized. This concept seeks to question current educational practices as a structural dilemma, presenting the contradiction between an official discourse of horizontality and concrete schooling, passive, and vertical practices that alienate students and reproduce social inequality. Finally, grounded in Paulo Freire's Pedagogy of the Question and within the framework of the Higher Education Law (Art. 43) and Ministerial Resolution 343/09, it proposes to denaturalize current university logics to move from a mere transmission of knowledge towards a committed praxis, aimed at producing critical and proactive professionals capable of responding to current social demands and promoting a more equitable society.

articles.title: The Moral Grammar of Magnifica Humanitas - A Discussion Paper
articles.abstract: On May 15th 2026, on the 135th anniversary of Rerum Novarum, Pope Leo XIV promulgated (published on May 25th) the encyclical Magnifica Humanitas — “On safeguarding the human person in the time of Artificial Intelligence.” Two months earlier (March 2026), the author had circulated, through SSRN and RePEc (DOI: 10.2139/ssrn.6468803), The Moral Grammar of Economics: An Empirical and Mathematical Framework for Jesuit-Inspired Social Justice. The present paper performs a systematic dialogue between the two texts: (i) it identifies the doctrinal claims of Magnifica Humanitas that admit a direct mathematical operationalization through the constraints, wedges, and welfare functions developed in the Moral Grammar; (ii) it isolates the novel doctrinal contributions introduced by Leo XIV — the Babel/Jerusalem dichotomy, the disarmament of words and code, the new monopolies of AI, the culture of care, and the explicit move beyond GDP — and formalises each as new terms or constraints within the welfare architecture; (iii) it re-anchors these formal extensions in the panel-data evidence already presented in the Moral Grammar (172 countries, 1963–2024), in particular the solidarity-response coefficient 𝛽=0.664 and the subsidiarity penalty 𝜎=0.856; (iv) it proposes a composite Magnifica Humanitas Index (MHI) for monitoring societies along the integral-human-development frontier; and (v) it translates the result into a concrete five-point policy program for the AI era. The fusion shows that Magnifica Humanitas is not only a theological document but also a precise research agenda for a humanist quantitative economics — one in which Jesuit-inspired ethical constraints become binding mathematical inequalities rather than ornamental preambles. This paper does not claim that the encyclical was influenced by the Moral Grammar; it claims only that the architectural alignment between the two texts is precise enough to be operationally useful, especially for the global network of Jesuit and Catholic universities engaged in the post-AI re-foundation of social-economic thought.

articles.title: Multilayer Psychographic Analysis of a Personal Facebook Audience: An LLM-Assisted Pipeline with Reliability Assessment
articles.abstract: Personal Facebook profiles host consequential publics that became effectively unmeasurable after the post-Cambridge-Analytica API lockdown. I describe an owner-authorized, API-less pipeline that recovers an engaged-audience network and a multilayer psychographic description of it from a single personal profile -- my own. The pipeline combines scrape-once/parse-many browser collection from the owner's session, two-pass LLM rubric scoring of 11,036 comments on 34 affective, moral, epistemic and political dimensions in their original languages, avatar vision scoring, topic labeling, co-engagement and reply graph construction, and the profile's own 21,199-follower dashboard as demographic ground truth. The central contribution is a validation protocol that treats the LLM as a measurement instrument: per-dimension test-retest reliability (18 of 33 dimensions pass an r >= 0.7 gate; two are retired), ordinal statistics, per-language stratification (no Russian-language penalty, with one flagged sub-gate cell), and cross-model convergence against a second frontier model family, which shows expressive-emotion constructs are model-robust while several moral-foundations dimensions are model-sensitive. A tiered de-identification design (keep / aggregate-only / drop) grounded in a k-anonymity scan yields a public release, and a 503-comment human validation with pre-registered decision rules is pending; all substantive findings are preliminary until it completes.
